text.title: SSL/TLS Setup Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the SSL/TLS Setup methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: TLS 1.3 MUST be the negotiated default. TLS 1.2 enabled only as fallback for legacy clients with documented justification. TLS 1.0/1.1/SSLv3 MUST be disabled.
rule.rationale: TLS 1.3 removes legacy attack vectors (RC4, CBC, weak DH); 1.0/1.1 are forbidden by PCI-DSS and most modern compliance frameworks.

rule.statement: Cipher suites limited to ECDHE-* + AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305. RSA key exchange, CBC modes, RC4, and DES are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Modern ciphers offer forward secrecy + AEAD; old ciphers are subject to known plaintext/oracle attacks.

rule.statement: Certificates MUST renew automatically ≥30 days before expiry via cert-manager + ACME. Manual renewal procedures are treated as control gaps.
rule.rationale: Manual renewal is the #1 cause of certificate-expired outages; auto-renew eliminates the human step.

rule.statement: Public web endpoints MUST emit HSTS header with max-age >= 31536000 (1y) + includeSubDomains + preload. Domain submitted to hstspreload.org.
rule.rationale: HSTS prevents downgrade to HTTP; preload removes the first-visit window.

rule.statement: OCSP stapling MUST be enabled on the TLS terminator. Client-side OCSP queries are forbidden as they leak browsing to the CA.
rule.rationale: Stapling closes the privacy leak + improves handshake latency.

rule.statement: Every TLS-touching change MUST run a post-deploy scan (testssl.sh / ssllabs-scan / SSL Labs API) and store the result in the change record. Grade < A is a blocking finding.
rule.rationale: Scanners catch the regressions humans miss: stale ciphers, missing HSTS, expired chain. Storing the scan creates an audit trail.
